item.title: Tokome appointed Lloyds Metals’ CEO
item.description: HULALA Tokome has been appointed chief executive officer of Lloyds Metals & Energy Limited (LMEL) for its Pacific initiatives, starting with the Autonomous Region of Bougainville effective July 1. LMEL managing director B. Prabhakaran said that Tokome, “apart from being an established industry leader, brings with him a personal connection to the region alongside a strong vision for sustainable
item.content:encoded:
HULALA Tokome has been appointed chief executive officer of Lloyds Metals & Energy Limited (LMEL) for its Pacific initiatives, starting with the Autonomous Region of Bougainville effective July 1.

LMEL managing director B. Prabhakaran said that Tokome, “apart from being an established industry leader, brings with him a personal connection to the region alongside a strong vision for sustainable economic development and community empowerment”.

“His leadership will be instrumental in advancing LMEL’s strategic interests while ensuring that development outcomes are inclusive, responsible, and aligned with the aspirations of the Bougainville people,” Prabhakaran said.

“Originally from Bougainville and Manus parentage, Tokome is a highly respected Papua New Guinean business leader with over three decades of experience across the energy, resources, and financial services sectors.”

Prabhakaran noted that this appointment reflects Lloyds’ commitment to building strong regional leadership to support its long-term development ambitions across the Pacific, commencing in Bougainville.

He said that Tokome would continue to serve as a non-executive director of the Puma Energy PNG Group of Companies, maintaining his 30-year association with the business where he has held several senior leadership roles, including as its country managing director and chairman.

“His contribution to the petroleum sector and superannuation industry has been widely recognised, including the award of an MBE,” he added.

Tokome currently serves as chairman of the Nasfund Contributors Savings & Loan Society and chairman of Mainland Holdings Limited.

Tokome said he was honoured to take on the role with Lloyds Metals & Energy “and to contribute meaningfully, particularly in Bougainville, which is home to me”.

“This opportunity allows me to combine my professional experience with my personal commitment to ensuring that our people benefit meaningfully from development. I look forward to working closely with all stakeholders to deliver sustainable and inclusive outcomes,” he said.

Lloyds Metals and Energy Limited (LMEL) is an India-based mining and metals company listed on the National Stock Exchange and Bombay Stock Exchange, India, with a market capitalisation of around US$9 billion (about K39 billion).

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PM hails ruling as step to boost telecommunication
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has welcomed the National Court’s decision on the Starlink licensing case, describing it as an important step in improving communication access in the country. “My Government welcomes this outcome because Papua New Guinea urgently needs reliable, real-time communication services for our people, businesses, schools, health facilities, and disaster response agencies,” Mar
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has welcomed the National Court’s decision on the Starlink licensing case, describing it as an important step in improving communication access in the country.

“My Government welcomes this outcome because Papua New Guinea urgently needs reliable, real-time communication services for our people, businesses, schools, health facilities, and disaster response agencies,” Marape said.

He said the decision allowed the country to move ahead with practical solutions to improve telecommunication services, especially for rural and remote communities that had struggled with poor or non-existent network coverage.

He said that the recent natural disasters, including cyclone-related emergencies affecting parts of the country, had demonstrated the importance of dependable communication systems.

“When communities are cut off during cyclones, floods, earthquakes, or other emergencies, lives can depend on real-time communication. We must ensure our people are never isolated in times of crisis,” he said.

Marape said the Starlink process had begun through lawful regulatory channels when the National Information and Communications Technology Authority (Nicta) began public consultation in 2023 on low earth-orbit satellite internet services, with major global industry players participating in the process.

“Today’s ruling restores certainty and confirms that proper regulatory institutions must be allowed to perform their lawful functions in the national interest,” he said.

“We support Starlink coming into PNG, alongside existing operators, because our people deserve better services, broader coverage, and more choices.”

Acting Minister for Information and Communications Technology, Peter Tsiamalili Jnr said the outcome reinforced the importance of compliance with national laws governing the ICT sector.

“Importantly, this decision is both timely and significant.

“It provides a clear and enabling pathway for the Nicta to consider licensing broadband services to support national disaster preparedness and response efforts,” he said.

“In a country characterised by challenging terrain, dispersed populations, and vulnerability to natural disasters, satellite connectivity presents a practical and strategic complement to existing terrestrial infrastructure.

“It enhances the Government’s ability to overcome geographic barriers and extend reliable communication services to remote and underserved communities.”

Meanwhile, Chief Secretary Ivan Pomaleu has urged Nicta to speed up the final approvals for telecommunication service providers such as Starlink.

This follows the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology announcement of a regulatory outcome that clears the path for the licensing of satellite broadband services, including the entry of Starlink into the PNG market.

Pomaleu welcomed the decision by Tsiamalili Jr as a win for national connectivity, disaster resilience, and the digital economy.

Pomaleu called on Nicta to finalise the licensing process of Starlink.

“This outcome affirms Papua New Guinea’s commitment to a transparent, rules-based regulatory environment,” he said.

“Our people in remote and underserved communities cannot afford to wait for the benefits of high-speed, reliable internet.”

Polmaleu said that areas where satellite licensing would have an impact included:

He said that the Government viewed the entry of global innovators such as Starlink as an opportunity to strengthen national resilience.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Treat Hela as frontline security zone, says Marape
item.description: HELA must be treated as a frontline security zone because of its importance to Papua New Guinea’s economy and national stability, says Prime Minister James Marape. He made the remarks during the opening of 32 police houses and the handover of 10 vehicles at the Enopi Police Barracks.
item.content:encoded:
HELA must be treated as a frontline security zone because of its importance to Papua New Guinea’s economy and national stability, says Prime Minister James Marape.

He made the remarks during the opening of 32 police houses and the handover of 10 vehicles at the Enopi Police Barracks.

The project was funded by the Hela government.

They were handed over to Police Minister Sir John Pundari in the presence of Police Commissioner David Manning.

Marape, the Tari Pori MP, said Hela was located at the centre of PNG’s economic engine.

“Hela is critical to our national economy and must be secured properly,” he said.

Marape said policing was a Government responsibility, and acknowledged the Hela government for stepping in to fund police housing and vehicles.

“The Government must match the investment with more manpower, logistics and operational support,” he said.

The 32 houses are part of the Hela government’s plan to build 500 homes for police personnel in the province.

This is to improve accommodation and strengthen long-term police presence.

Sir John said the new houses set a benchmark for police accommodation in the country.

“The standard has now been set. These houses are fully equipped with white goods, brown goods and solar power, and must be properly maintained by officers,” he said,

Sir John said discipline in the force began with how officers lived and maintained their environment.

Manning said the Enopi rollout was part of reforms to rebuild the constabulary, with Hela identified as a priority province due to ongoing security challenges.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Plea for action in Kokoda as number of deaths rises
item.description: EIGHTEEN persons have died since the beginning of the year while many more are still fighting different curable diseases in Kokoda local level government (LLG), Sohe, Northern, according to an official. Rumara Primary School head teacher Denis Naita said almost 10,000 people in the Emo, Wawanga valley in Kokoda LLG were affected.
item.content:encoded:
EIGHTEEN persons have died since the beginning of the year while many more are still fighting different curable diseases in Kokoda local level government (LLG), Sohe, Northern, according to an official.

Rumara Primary School head teacher Denis Naita said almost 10,000 people in the Emo, Wawanga valley in Kokoda LLG were affected.

“The population there depends entirely on Emo Health Centre which was closed early this year because of medical drugs shortages,” he said.

“This led to the deaths that happened from January through to April.”

It is understood that the order for medical drugs to the health centre had been placed at the provincial authority in Popondetta since January, but the medicines have yet to arrive at the health facility which is still closed.

Naita said Emo health centre serve ward 21 (Wora), ward 22 (Emo), ward 23 (Awoma) and ward 24 (Duau) in Kokoda LLG.

“The last person to die because there were no medicines and the health centre was closed was my brother who passed away from acute malaria,” he said.

“We were transporting him on stretcher for two days and a night to catch a PMV (public motor vehicle) to Popondetta general hospital when he died on April 15, so we had to bring his body back home and buried him the next day.” 

According to Naita, the nearest health centre is Sirorata and people in those wards can reach it after a day’s walk through difficult terrain.

He said of the 18 deaths, three were women, two babies and the rest men.

These are the figures for wards 21 and 22, while wards 23 and 24 are unaccounted for at this stage.

Naita said some who died were young fathers who had left behind children and wives.

“We need our LLG, district and provincial authorities to assist us. It is an urgent call,” he said.

“The provincial government also needs to consider building road access to Wawanga valley to ease the day-to-day struggles that people are facing.”

Naita added that if action was not taken immediately by the authorities, more people were likely to succumb to curable diseases and conditions in the coming months.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Jarome Luai visits Chiefs HQ
item.description: WESTS Tigers superstar Jarome Luai is in Papua New Guinea , indicating that the Samoan five-eighth is interested in a move to the 19th NRL franchise. With current clauses in his contract in Luai’s favour, he would be available to sign with the PNG franchise when they enter the competition in 2028.
item.content:encoded:
WESTS Tigers superstar Jarome Luai is in Papua New Guinea , indicating that the Samoan five-eighth is interested in a move to the 19th NRL franchise.

With current clauses in his contract in Luai’s favour, he would be available to sign with the PNG franchise when they enter the competition in 2028.

With tax-free benefits incentivising players to make the move, it could see Luai as a franchise signing for the Chiefs.

It is being reported by The Age that Luai’s visit was in the company of the PNG Chiefs chief executive officer Lorna McPherson and general manager Michael Chammas as they try to get him over the line.

He has until April 30 to decide whether he will stay at the Tigers, otherwise, it could be an all-out war for Luai’s signature.

Gated communities, paradise living accommodation, tax-free earnings, and access to private island resorts spearhead the incentives for players to relocate to Papua New Guinea.

The Chiefs would be setting up their maiden year with great confidence if they can land a superstar signing in Luai to kick-off their legacy.

Not only would Luai be a key figure in the Chiefs’ spine, but he would be an ambassador and the face of the franchise amongst the country.

The potential marquee signing would see Luai leave the Tigers three years into his five-year deal.

The idea of tax-free is likely to lure alot of NRL stars to play for the Chief when they enter NRL in 2028. – Zero Tackle

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Ten pay K1.5mil total rego
item.description: TEN franchise clubs have paid a total of K1.5mil to officially confirm their participation in the upcoming Premier Soccer League (PSL) season, set to kick off next month in Port Moresby and Lae. The PNG Football Association (PNGFA) said each of the clubs have met mandatory regulatory requirements, completing full compliance with registration through the payment of the expression of interest (EOI)
item.content:encoded:
TEN franchise clubs have paid a total of K1.5mil to officially confirm their participation in the upcoming Premier Soccer League (PSL) season, set to kick off next month in Port Moresby and Lae.

The PNG Football Association (PNGFA) said each of the clubs have met mandatory regulatory requirements, completing full compliance with registration through the payment of the expression of interest (EOI) non-refundable fee of K20,000 and the K130,000 affiliation fee, totaling K150,000 per club.

The confirmed clubs for the 2026 season are Gulf Komara FC, Agmark FC Morobe Wawens, Lae City FC, PNG Hekari FC, Port Moresby Strikers FC, Kula FC, Admiralty Islands FC, Madang FC, WAWA FC, and NCD FC.

With the roster finalised and financially cleared, PNGFA’s competition department is now in the final stages of preparing the official season draw and match-day operations. PNGFA chief operating officer Stanley Khanna commended the competition department for its diligent collaboration with the franchise clubs, noting that as the PSL season prepares for kick-off, PNGFA invites fans and sponsors nationwide to be part of what promises to be an exciting season.

Khanna said PSL continues to grow as a platform for talent, community pride, and partnership, and PNGFA looks forward to delivering a competition that everyone could be proud of.

He said further details on fixtures and the official launch would soon be announced.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Equaliser secures U16 spot for Cooks
item.description: DESPITE an improved performance on home soil at the PNG Football Stadium, Papua New Guinea’s Junior Bilums fell short of qualification as the Cook Islands secured top spot in the qualifying tournament with a resilient 1-1 draw. Heading into the clash, both sides were level with one win apiece after dominant victories over American Samoa. However, Cook Islands held the edge on goal difference and o
item.content:encoded:
DESPITE an improved performance on home soil at the PNG Football Stadium, Papua New Guinea’s Junior Bilums fell short of qualification as the Cook Islands secured top spot in the qualifying tournament with a resilient 1-1 draw.

Heading into the clash, both sides were level with one win apiece after dominant victories over American Samoa. However, Cook Islands held the edge on goal difference and only needed a draw to advance, while PNG required a win to claim the lone qualification spot.

PNG started brightly, moving the ball quickly and probing for openings. Striker Delma Duadak nearly broke the deadlock in the 15th minute, but Cook Islands defender Mamia Piri-Savage intervened decisively. The visitors threatened from set-pieces, with Teniva Tauu firing over after confusion from a corner.

The breakthrough came in the 34th minute when Oscalina Gunemba orchestrated a slick move that Joylin Lisah finished clinically, giving PNG a 1-0 halftime lead.

Cook Islands responded with urgency after the break.

A turning point arrived when PNG’s Serah Witu was booked, leading to a dangerous free-kick. The equaliser followed in the 63rd minute when Allana Vino fouled Tehanarei Nga in the box, and Metuamaru Arere converted the penalty with composure.

With momentum shifting, Cook Islands pressed forward while PNG pushed bodies into attack late on. Ultimately, the visitors held firm to secure the draw they needed, sealing qualification for the OFC Under-16 Women’s Championship in Honiara, Solomon Islands.

The result capped a disciplined and resilient display from Cook Islands, while PNG were left to rue missed opportunities despite showing promise at home.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PETE’S PUNCHLINE
item.description: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
item.content:encoded: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Starlink to bridge the divide
item.description: THIS is a landmark moment for Papua New Guinea, one that deserves celebration by all who value progress, inclusion, and the transformative power of technology. The National Court’s decision to quash the Ombudsman Commission’s directive affirms the rule of law and protects independent regulators in carrying out their constitutional mandate.
item.content:encoded:
THIS is a landmark moment for Papua New Guinea, one that deserves celebration by all who value progress, inclusion, and the transformative power of technology.

The National Court’s decision to quash the Ombudsman Commission’s directive affirms the rule of law and protects independent regulators in carrying out their constitutional mandate.

By upholding the National Information and Communications Technology Authority’s authority over telecommunications and digital services, the court has reinforced the institutional stability and legal clarity that investors and innovators need to bring new technology into PNG.

With Starlink’s licensing able to proceed, we are on the verge of a turning point in digital connectivity.

For too long, geography has left thousands of communities on the wrong side of the digital divide, limiting access to education, healthcare, markets, and government services.

Low-earth orbit satellite technology can overcome these barriers, delivering high-speed, low-latency internet directly to schools, villages, aid posts, and small businesses.

This ruling restores momentum to PNG’s digital agenda, enabling e-learning, e-health, digital identity, and public service delivery nationwide.

Students, farmers, nurses, and entrepreneurs all stand to benefit.

Today, the path to a connected, inclusive, and innovative PNG is clearer, and it is up to all of us to turn this opportunity into lasting change.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Scrolling affects concentration
item.description: THE digital age has put the world at our fingertips, but it may be eroding our capacity for deep thought. Walk into a train carriage or college lounge today, and screens dominate every gaze.
item.content:encoded:
THE digital age has put the world at our fingertips, but it may be eroding our capacity for deep thought.

Walk into a train carriage or college lounge today, and screens dominate every gaze.

While the “Zoomer” generation is extraordinarily tech-savvy, educators and cognitive scientists warn that the art of sustained, linear reading is fading.

Scrolling through TikTok , X , and news feeds trains the brain to skim, scanning for keywords and skipping details.

This is efficient for emails, but disastrous for a 400-page novel, where the lack of immediate gratification feels mentally exhausting.

Electronic devices also exploit dopamine, offering constant rewards for brief engagement, while books demand patience and delayed satisfaction.

Moreover, hyperlinks and fragmented content encourage disjointed thinking, making it harder to follow a complex narrative from start to finish.

Without the ability to sustain focus, building nuanced understanding or a sophisticated worldview becomes challenging.

The solution isn’t abandoning technology, but practising “digital fasting”.

Young people must relearn to sit quietly with a book, exercising the intellectual stamina that devices rarely demand.

Deep reading is not just a hobby; it is essential training for the human mind in an age built for distraction.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Coffee plant promises lucrative returns
item.description: UNDER Governor Simon Sia’s leadership, the Eastern Highlands government has launched a ground-breaking initiative: an instant coffee processing plant. This marks a major shift in the coffee industry by aiming to increase profits for local farmers and eliminate the so-called “middlemen” who often benefit more than the producers.
item.content:encoded:
UNDER Governor Simon Sia’s leadership, the Eastern Highlands government has launched a ground-breaking initiative: an instant coffee processing plant.

This marks a major shift in the coffee industry by aiming to increase profits for local farmers and eliminate the so-called “middlemen” who often benefit more than the producers.

The establishment of this plant addresses a long-standing issue – previous leaders overlooked the potential of directly processing coffee in the region.

Now, with the plant in place, local farmers can expect higher earnings as they bypass intermediaries and sell directly to the processing facility.

The project, which includes the construction of the plant and a residential home for the manager, was built at a cost of K15 million, funded by the Eastern Highlands government.

This investment promises a more lucrative future for local coffee farmers and is seen as a positive step for the province’s economic development.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Football team has potential to improve
item.description: THE Papua New Guinea team that played against the Cook Islands in the OFC Under-16 Women’s Championship qualifying match, which ended in a 1-1 draw on Friday, needs targeted development to become more competitive. Unfortunately, despite the draw, the PNG girls will not advance to face other OFC teams later this year.
item.content:encoded:
THE Papua New Guinea team that played against the Cook Islands in the OFC Under-16 Women’s Championship qualifying match, which ended in a 1-1 draw on Friday, needs targeted development to become more competitive.

Unfortunately, despite the draw, the PNG girls will not advance to face other OFC teams later this year.

PNG took the lead after 36 minutes and held it into halftime, but the Cook Islands, boosted by their New Zealand-based players, dominated the centre of the field in the second half.

Their strategic set-piece plays allowed them to equalise from a spot kick, denying PNG control.

It is my view that PNG could have used the left flank more effectively, creating chances for forward Delma Duadak, who received a few quality balls and struggled to showcase her pace against larger Polynesian defenders.

 Many PNG players are experiencing their first major international match, and they need development in tactical awareness, positional play, and ball distribution to compete at this level.

Particularly, players on the left flank should be nurtured to strengthen their impact.

The potential is clear, and with strategic guidance from the PNG Football Association, these young players can be prepared for future international competitions.

We wish them all the best in their development and journey.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Defending humanity and freedom of speech amid growing impunity
item.description: As the “Gaza Playbook” seemingly extends to Lebanon, including attacks on critical civilian infrastructure, humanitarian personnel and journalists, we present this summary of a December 2025 diascussion between panellists Amra Lee, Dr Cadhla O’Sullivan, Dr Annabel Dulhunty, Dr Ali Shaar, Dr Richard Brennan and Mudasser Siddiqui. In December last year, academics and practitioners came together at t
item.content:encoded:
As the “Gaza Playbook” seemingly extends to Lebanon, including attacks on critical civilian infrastructure, humanitarian personnel and journalists, we present this summary of a December 2025 diascussion between panellists Amra Lee, Dr Cadhla O’Sullivan, Dr Annabel Dulhunty, Dr Ali Shaar, Dr Richard Brennan and Mudasser Siddiqui.

In December last year, academics and practitioners came together at the 2025 Australasian Aid and International Development Conference to discuss human rights and humanitarian issues in Gaza.

While a ceasefire had technically been negotiated in October last year, over 500 violations had been documented as of November, alongside intensifying settler violence and annexation in the West Bank.

Opening the panel, Dr Annabel Dulhunty from the Australian National University (ANU) highlighted the problem of “a ceasefire that is not a ceasefire” and the challenge of universities shying away from discussions on Gaza.

Dr Ali Nashat Shaar, from An Najah University in the West Bank, underlined that an 18-year-old in Gaza today had already survived six wars.

Dr Cadhla O’Sullivan (ANU) described how all children’s rights had been violated, their existence reduced to mere survival.

Mudasser Siddiqui from Plan International said that, despite all this, after the ceasefire announcement, children in Gaza were sharing hope for the future.

Six months later, that ceasefire has failed to meet its own protection and humanitarian objectives, according to a coalition of non-government organisations.

Dr Rick Brennan, former WHO Regional Emergency Director for the World Health Organization (WHO) in the Eastern Mediterranean Region, and Amra Lee, practitioner and PhD researcher (ANU), unpacked the humanitarian consequences of Gaza and beyond – from over 70,000 deaths and 170,000 injuries, to record aid-worker deaths, hundreds of attacks on healthcare, soaring humanitarian needs and dramatic reductions in global funding.

They outlined the major operational constraints on the United Nations (UN)-led aid-delivery system, including the prevention of United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East (UNRWA) from bringing personnel and aid into Gaza.

Moreover, the establishment of the Gaza Humanitarian Foundation (GHF) during an unfolding famine demonstrated the risks of replacing principled aid delivery with inexperienced, politicised contractors during a complex and volatile humanitarian operation – risks that saw 1,397 Palestinians killed and another 4,000 injured trying to reach GHF food distribution sites.

At the session’s close, the panellists and interlocutors in the audience shared a collective sigh of sadness and relief – that it was possible to have a critical and respectful dialogue on Gaza.

That senior experts felt this tension in speaking about human suffering in Gaza, based on UN reports and data, requires critical reflection.

As Australian academic Na’ama Carlin has highlighted, there is a Gaza-shaped hole in curricula that has had far-reaching implications for Palestinians since Oct 7 2023.

Many educators have avoided discussing Gaza due to fear and lack of institutional support.

This fear has been exacerbated by pressure on institutions to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition of antisemitism – a definition not intended to be legally binding. Universities Australia adopted an IHRA-aligned definition in February last year.

The IHRA definition risks being interpreted and applied in ways that would prevent legitimate criticism of Israel’s conduct with respect to human rights and international law, exacerbating the chilling effect on academic freedom.

ANU previously declined to adopt this definition in 2023 and last year; its current position remains unclear.

Following the December 2025 extremist attack on a Jewish celebration in Bondi, intense political pressure resulted in a rushed government response – including the adoption of the IHRA definition.

This was accompanied by federal and state legislation that legal experts have assessed as unreasonably restricting the right to protest and freedom of expression.

From the unravelling of writers’ festivals to the use of force against protesters in Sydney, these measures have operated, in effect, to silence discussion of and protest against human suffering in one of the world’s most violent conflicts and severe humanitarian crisis.

Without greater political leadership and moral courage, such responses jeopardise the critical importance of addressing the rise both of antisemitism and Islamophobia, alongside all forms of racism and discrimination in Australia, including against First Nations people.

The Australian Human Rights Commission’s report on racism at Australian universities found interpersonal and structural racism “deeply embedded” in the sector.

This reinforces the urgency of identifying and responding to the drivers and enablers of all forms of racism and discrimination, consistent with human rights principles of equality and non-discrimination.

Australian universities have undergone significant change over the past three decades.

Dramatic funding cuts have shifted institutional objectives, leading to growing pressure for a reckoning.

The societal costs are visible in responses to student protests and a broader erosion of support for the physical and intellectual spaces for necessary, difficult conversations on Gaza and racism.

This has contributed to a decrease in public trust in universities, with a recent survey showing the majority of Australians expect more from our learning institutions than making a profit.

Our panel made a modest but important contribution: holding space to discuss Gaza and the wider implications of continued impunity for humanity.

The lack of consequences for sustained attacks on the UN in Gaza has emboldened adoption of the same patterns in Lebanon, including attacks on healthcare, food systems, humanitarian personnel and journalists, and dangerous disinformation campaigns that equate funding the UN with support for proscribed groups.

item.title: Learning Chinese a plus for Ryan
item.description: UNITECH student Ryan Bagah is looking forward to making a third trip to China – thanks to his efforts to learn the Chinese language on campus in Lae. “What motivated me the most was the advantages of learning Chinese, such as being able to communicate with people from different backgrounds, and having a good chance to study in China. It pushed me to keep going.”
item.content:encoded:
UNITECH student Ryan Bagah is looking forward to making a third trip to China – thanks to his efforts to learn the Chinese language on campus in Lae.

“What motivated me the most was the advantages of learning Chinese, such as being able to communicate with people from different backgrounds, and having a good chance to study in China. It pushed me to keep going.”

Ryan, 23, who recently graduated from the School of Mathematics and Computer Science at Unitech, has travelled to China twice already and is looking forward to his third trip where he will study at a university there.

His parents are from East Sepik and Western Highlands. He is the only boy in a family of four.

He completed his high school in 2021 at Fr. Peter Secondary in Banz, Jiwaka.

“I love learning foreign language during my free time. I watched YouTube videos and try practising it myself. That is, until I found out about the Confucius Institute run by a Chinese in campus. He taught the Chinese language to students.”

Ryan registered and started Chinese classes in 2024 while doing his third-year Mathematics and Computer Science course.

At first, he found learning Chinese very difficult. But over time he started to build an interest in understanding it.

Apart from speaking Chinese, Ryan can also speak a little bit of France and German both of which he learned online.

“One of the biggest challenges I faced was balancing my university studies with learning Chinese. It required a lot of discipline and time management, especially during exam time and when loaded with assignments.

“However, I stayed consistent and kept practising. It helped me improve steadily over time. I also want to prove myself that I could succeed in something outside of my main field of study.”

His first trip to China was in April last year. After returning home, he won a Chinese bridge competition on campus in May. In August, he returned to China to represent PNG in a Chinese bridge competition.

Later, he was also awarded a scholarship to study Chinese at any university in China.

 “I plan to take up the scholarship offer and return to Chongqing Normal University to study Hanyu Shuiping Kaoshi (HSK) 4 and 5 in March next year. The scholarship covers everything except air travel. So I’m saving up for that.”

HSK is the international standardized test for Chinese language proficiency that is offered at the Unitech campus. They only teach HSK 1 to 3. HSK 4 to 5 can be studied in China.

He thanked the Confucius Institute in Unitech for the great opportunity and experience that allowed him to meet so many different people from all over the world and provide him the opportunity to study overseas.

“I am proud of everything I’ve accomplished so far with the hope of achieving even more in the future.

It’s just a matter of discipline and commitment. Anyone can do anything; you just have to believe in yourself and believe in God.”

There is a saying in Chinese which, loosely translated, means: “Learning is like sailing against the current: if you don’t advance, you will retreat.” It highlights the need for continuous effort. It also emphasizes that Mandarin requires daily, active study to avoid losing progress.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: It’s time to mandate environmental spending in Indonesia
item.description: Indonesia’s flash floods in Sumatra are not merely natural disasters; they are also symptomatic of fiscal failure. While rescue teams battled torrents of mud, a quieter tragedy unfolded in the budget books.
item.content:encoded:
Indonesia’s flash floods in Sumatra are not merely natural disasters; they are also symptomatic of fiscal failure.

While rescue teams battled torrents of mud, a quieter tragedy unfolded in the budget books.

Disaster mitigation funding was systematically crowded out by competing political agendas.

It signals a concerning reality that, within Indonesia’s current fiscal hierarchy, resilience appears expendable until a disaster hits.

The crisis extends far beyond emergency response budgets.

As climate change accelerates, global norms dictate that nations should ramp up environmental spending to keep risks within acceptable limits.

These funds are critical for reforestation and upgrading ecological defences before disaster strikes.

The ministry of finance reveals a chilling fiscal paradox: 236 local governments slashed their environmental spending last year compared to the previous year, widening Indonesia’s climate finance gap, which is estimated to reach US$145 billion (about K640 billion) through 2030.

Worse still, even among districts that do allocate environmental budgets, spending bears almost no relationship to environmental quality, suggesting that the problem runs deeper than underfunding alone (see Figure 1).

The ministry of finance has attempted to steer local budgets toward environmental priorities using soft fiscal instruments.

Under Law No.1 of 2022 on Central and Regional Financial Relations, land cover indices are now factored into the formula for the general allocation fund – the main unconditional transfer from Jakarta to the regions – theoretically rewarding areas that preserve their forests.

The law also refines Revenue Sharing Funds – transfers that redistribute natural resource royalties – by compensating areas suffering from the negative externalities of resource extraction in neighbouring regions.

Furthermore, the Special Allocation Fund – a conditional grant for priority sectors – explicitly directs capital into environmental maintenance.

Yet these mechanisms remain largely incentives rather than binding mandates, leaving execution at the mercy of local political will.

The liquidity trap becomes evident when examining international climate finance as an alternative.

Most results-based instruments, such as those from the Green Climate Fund, operate on a reimbursement model where local governments must front costs and receive payments only after verified results are achieved.

Cash-constrained regions cannot sustain multi-year environmental interventions while awaiting reimbursement cycles that span several months or even years.

This funding asymmetry collides with what might be called a conservation paradox, though the reality is more complex than a simple trade-off between forests and growth.

The six Papua provinces, Indonesia’s easternmost frontier, maintain forest cover exceeding 80 per cent and harbour globally significant carbon stores.

Yet the region also records Indonesia’s highest poverty rate – around 26 per cent – despite receiving billions in special autonomy transfers and hosting the massive Grasberg copper and gold mine, one of the world’s largest.

Much of Papua’s forest cover persists not because of deliberate conservation policy, but because of remoteness and limited infrastructure.

At the same time, extraction is actively expanding: new permits for nickel mining, palm oil plantations and logging are encroaching on primary forest, with deforestation rates rising.

The paradox, then, is not that Papua has chosen conservation over development, but that neither the revenues from extraction nor the fiscal transfers from Jakarta have translated into meaningful local welfare – while the pressure to open more forest only grows.

Meanwhile, Sumatra and Kalimantan converted vast forest tracts into oil palm, timber and coal extraction zones decades ago, fuelling rapid economic growth.

Without deliberate fiscal intervention, conservation-rich regions will remain cash-starved, perpetuating a system where environmental stewardship is a burden only the poorest are expected to bear.

Given this reality, mandating environmental expenditure is not only justified but necessary.

A binding floor would force local leaders to allocate environmental budgets at a specified minimum, with failure to meet targets triggering fiscal penalties in future transfers – mirroring the existing 20 per cent education mandate.

The Supreme Audit Institution of Indonesia identified trillions of rupiah lost to governmental inefficiency in its 2024 financial report.

Beyond this, local budgets also suffer chronic bloat from inflated payroll costs that crowd out productive expenditures.

Law 1/2022 already mandates capping personnel expenses at 30 per cent of regional budgets over coming years.
